(Ref: 36), Springer Nature. b, Differential expression analysis comparing cfRNA and matched leukocyte RNA from healthy donors (n = 10 pairs). Significance was determined using Wald test and adjusted using Benjamini–Hochberg correction. c, GSEA using cell-type-specific signatures from PanglaoDB (Ref: 12) (n = 170 cell types). The top 15 positively and negatively enriched cell types are shown, and the dashed line delineates significant enrichment. GI, gastrointestinal. d, Relationship between centrifugation speed during plasma isolation and cfRNA concentration per millilitre plasma (n = 10 at 1,200g, n = 6 at 1,800g and n = 16 at 2,500g). Comparisons were performed using the Kruskal–Wallis test. e, Relationship between centrifugation speed and platelet-specific gene expression in cfRNA samples from panel d. Comparisons were performed using the Kruskal–Wallis test. Average log2NX, mean of normalized expression. f, Principal component analysis of cfRNA expression clusters according to spin speed (PC1) and sex (PC2). The top 1,000 variable genes in cfRNA samples from panel d were used for principal component analysis. g, Association between average platelet-specific gene expression and PC1 in cfRNA samples from panel d before correction. Pearson and Spearman correlation are shown. h, Association between average platelet-specific gene expression and PC1 when considering only samples processed using the same centrifugation speed (n = 16 at 2,500g) before correction. i, Schematic of the platelet-directed correction approach (Methods). SVD, singular value decomposition; W, platelet correction factor; Z, z scores; Zp, z scores of platelet genes. j, Association between platelet-specific gene expression and PC1 after platelet-directed correction. k, Hierarchical clustering of the top 1,000 variable genes in cfRNA before correction. Clustering was performed using Euclidean distance. l, Hierarchical clustering of the top 1,000 variable genes after platelet-directed correction. In the boxplots, the centre line denotes the median, the box contains the interquartile range (IQR), and the whiskers denote extrema no farther than 1.5× IQR.

### Subsection: Enhancing sensitivity of ctRNA detection

Although cfRNA is highly enriched for expression of genes from non-haematopoietic sources, the absolute expression levels of these genes are relatively low compared with haematopoietic-derived transcripts (for example, globins) and highly expressed housekeeping genes (for example, β-actin). We therefore hypothesized that selectively capturing genes absent from or lowly expressed in healthy control cfRNA could enhance detection of non-haematopoietic tissue or disease signatures and expand the utility of RARE-seq to a wide range of clinical applications. To test this, we identified such ‘rare abundance genes’ (RAGs) using whole-coding transcriptome RARE-seq data from healthy cfRNA samples (n = 50) and RNA-seq data from healthy whole-blood samples (n = 307) from the GTEx project (Ref: 16) (Fig. 2a). We defined RAGs based on consistent low or absent expression in cfRNA and whole blood (n = 5,632 genes; Extended Data Fig. 3a and Supplementary Table 1). As compared with all protein-coding transcripts, RAGs were substantially enriched for both non-haematopoietic tissue-specific genes and cancer-specific genes from diverse cancers (Fig. 2b,c).
The enrichment of genes specific for multiple tissue and cancer types among RAGs suggests that a RAG-targeted sequencing panel could increase sensitivity for detection of cancer or other conditions of interest without requiring a bespoke panel design for each application. We therefore designed a capture panel targeting 4,737 RAGs and 50 housekeeping genes, prioritizing genes with relatively uniform expression across healthy cfRNA and whole-blood samples while covering a broad dynamic range of expression (Extended Data Fig. 3b). Although these modules comprise the core of the RAG-focused capture panel design and could be used by themselves to explore diverse pathophysiological conditions without further customization, additional targets may be optionally added to the capture panel if desired (Extended Data Fig. 3c). For example, for the purposes of this study, we added genes known to be recurrently mutated in lung cancers (n = 122 genes), including genes such as TP53, KRAS, and RET (Extended Data Fig. 3d; Methods). The full collection of captured genes is listed in Supplementary Table 2.
We next profiled five healthy cfRNA samples using both whole-coding transcriptome and RAG capture panels using RARE-seq. Gene expression was highly correlated between matched libraries (R = 0.98, P ≤ 2.2 × 10−16; Extended Data Fig. 3e), including for the housekeeping genes (R = 0.97, P ≤ 2.2 × 10−16; Extended Data Fig. 3f). Within the subset of genes shared across both capture panels, RAG capture resulted in higher unique sequencing depth (Fig. 2d), more genes detected (Fig. 2e) and higher unique sequencing depth per gene detected (Fig. 2f). Thus, targeted capture of RAGs effectively increases sensitivity for detection of genes that are lowly expressed in healthy cfRNA.
We next set out to determine the limit of 95% detection (LOD95) for transcriptional signatures of interest, using either whole-coding transcriptome or RAG capture panels. To score the presence of a gene signature of interest in cfRNA samples, we developed an analytical framework to compute a cfRNA gene signature enrichment score by comparing the expression of pre-defined signature genes in a sample of interest to a ‘meta-reference’ consisting of cfRNA samples from healthy controls and using bootstrapping to estimate its significance (Fig. 2g; Methods). An important advantage of this approach is that unlike machine learning-based methods, it does not require a large training cohort of patient cfRNA samples, as it leverages existing gene expression datasets to define gene signatures and associated gene-wise weights based on their expression levels in the tissue or condition of interest.
To establish the LOD95 of RARE-seq, we generated in silico serial dilutions of RNA from the NCI-H1975 NSCLC cell line into healthy control cfRNA at various cancer fractions. To enable using our cfRNA enrichment score framework, we generated an NCI-H1975-specific gene signature, comprising the top differentially expressed genes (DEGs) distinguishing NCI-H1975 from a meta-reference healthy cfRNA profile (H1975 Sig; n = 122 genes). Using this signature, the LOD95 for RAG RARE-seq was 0.05% (Fig. 2h), more than 50-fold more sensitive than for whole-coding transcriptome RARE-seq (2.8%; Fig. 2i), demonstrating the value of targeting RAGs. To experimentally confirm this result, we created in vitro dilution series, by spiking NCI-H1975 RNA into healthy donor cfRNA at defined concentrations. These in vitro spikes demonstrated strong agreement with our in silico results (Fig. 2h,i). We also evaluated the effect of sequencing coverage depth (range of 10–50 million reads) on the LOD95 of RAG-targeted RARE-seq and found that higher sequencing depth improved the LOD95 (Extended Data Fig. 3i). Accordingly, we targeted 50 million reads for subsequent experiments using RARE-seq with the RAG panel.

In the boxplots, the centre line denotes the median, the box contains the IQR, and the whiskers denote extrema no farther than 1.5× IQR.

### Subsection: Detection of non-small-cell lung cancer

Having developed and optimized RARE-seq, we focused the remainder of our study on generating proof-of-concept data for cfRNA analysis across several clinical applications. Given the higher analytic sensitivity observed by capturing RAGs, we used RAG-focused RARE-seq for these experiments. First, we investigated performance for tumour-naive detection of lung adenocarcinomas (LUADs), the most common type of non-small-cell lung cancer (NSCLC). We analysed cfRNA from 139 patients with LUAD (n = 20 patients with stage I, n = 8 patients with stage II, n = 15 patients with stage III and n = 96 patients with stage IV) and 50 controls without cancer, including 26 risk-matched controls with a history of substantial tobacco exposure collected at the time of low-dose computed tomography (LDCT) screening for lung cancer. Concentrations of cfRNA and sequencing depth were similar between groups (Extended Data Fig. 4a,b). Platelet-adjusted differential expression analysis between LUADs and controls identified 96 genes overexpressed in LUADs, including key genes known to be highly expressed in LUADs such as SLC34A2, SFTA2 and NKX2-1 (also known as TTF1; Fig. 3a). LUAD DEGs were confirmed to be highly expressed in LUAD tumours from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) (Ref: 17), whereas were depleted in meta-reference cfRNA controls (Fig. 3b). In addition, using cell-type-specific gene signatures, GSEA revealed LUAD cfRNA to be most enriched for epithelial cells including pulmonary alveolar type II cells, and most depleted for naive and memory B lymphocytes (Fig. 3c). Thus, cfRNA from patients with LUAD is enriched for transcripts that are highly expressed in LUAD tumours.
To further quantify the enrichment of LUAD-derived transcripts, we applied the cfRNA enrichment score framework developed above using a LUAD-specific gene signature (LUAD Sig; n = 72 genes) identified using RNA-seq data from LUAD tumours within TCGA (Ref: 17), LUAD cell lines from the Cancer Cell Line Encyclopedia (CCLE) (Ref: 18), whole blood (GTEx (Ref: 16)) and meta-reference cfRNA controls (Methods). The LUAD Sig was detected in 101 out of 139 participants with LUAD (73% sensitivity at 95% specificity in non-cancer controls; Fig. 3d and Supplementary Table 3) and was significantly associated with stage (for sensitivity by stage, I = 30%, II = 62%, III = 67% and IV = 83%; P = 9.1 × 10−16; Fig. 3e). In addition, in a subset of plasma samples that were also interrogated using tumour-naive ctDNA analysis (n = 38; Methods), the LUAD cfRNA enrichment score was significantly correlated with ctDNA variant allele frequency (R = 0.43, P = 0.009; Fig. 3f). Of these samples, 34% was detected by only RARE-seq and none was detected by only ctDNA analysis (Fig. 3g), indicating that tumour-naive ctRNA analysis is more sensitive than tumour-naive ctDNA analysis. Technical variables that varied across groups, such as library input and unique sequencing depth, extraction method and capture panel, were not associated with sensitivity (Extended Data Fig. 4e–h), indicating that these variables did not drive detection. In addition, detection was not significantly associated with biological factors such as patient smoking status or the presence of oncogenic driver mutations (Extended Data Fig. 4i–k). Finally, we found that platelet correction significantly improved sensitivity (P = 0.03; Extended Data Fig. 4l).

### Subsection: Genotyping of somatic mutations in ctRNA

We also explored the ability to genotype cancer-derived somatic mutations using RARE-seq. Although non-invasive tumour genotyping is already routinely performed clinically using ctDNA and ctRNA-based genotyping is unlikely to outperform it, variant detection could be a useful addition to cfRNA analysis. We therefore developed a custom ctRNA genotyping approach to detect clinically actionable variants, including single-nucleotide variants (SNVs), insertions or deletions (indels), splice variants and gene fusions, in recurrent NSCLC oncogenes. To ensure specificity, putative variants were censored if their variant allele frequency did not significantly exceed the background error rate observed in a subset of withheld controls (n = 50; Extended Data Fig. 5a; Methods). Performance was assessed in 136 patients with stage IV LUAD with known variants from previous tumour or ctDNA genotyping, as well as 97 healthy and LDCT controls (Supplementary Table 4). We detected one or more somatic driver mutations in 28% of patients with LUAD and 1% of controls, including key NSCLC oncogenic drivers such as L858R and exon 19 deletions in EGFR, G12C in KRAS, ROS1 and RET fusions, and MET exon skipping events (Fig. 3h,i and Extended Data Fig. 5b–d). Of the 162 variants identified by tumour tissue genomic DNA or ctDNA genotyping in the same patients, 35 (22%) were also detectable in ctRNA, and detection rates were similar across variant types (Fig. 3j). Of the 34 variants identified by tumour tissue genomic DNA in patients with matched cfRNA and cfDNA data available, 11 variants were detectable in ctDNA compared with 3 variants detectable in ctRNA, indicating that ctRNA genotyping may be useful to supplement expression-based analyses but should not replace ctDNA genotyping (Extended Data Fig. 5e). We observed a significant relationship between variant detection rates and corresponding expression levels; for instance, EGFR variants were more frequently detected in cfRNA samples with high EGFR expression (P = 0.0001; Fig. 3k). Thus, RARE-seq analysis allows simultaneous identification of somatic mutations in patients with cancer.
Given that ctRNA expression signatures and ctRNA somatic variation could individually distinguish between patients with cancer and controls without cancer, we next investigated whether a machine learning approach could combine these features into a single detection algorithm. We trained a weighted elastic net classifier to distinguish LUAD and control cfRNA using tenfold nested cross-validation in a training cohort of 96 stage IV cases and 47 controls. The model demonstrated high classification performance (area under receiver operating characteristic curve, AUROC = 0.94; Fig. 3l,m and Supplementary Table 5) and selected 23 features including gene expression features (for example, key LUAD markers such as SLC34A2) and mutation-based features (that is, presence of L858R in EGFR or a KRAS mutation; Extended Data Fig. 5f). To test whether our cohort was sufficiently large, we repeated classifier training and cross-validation using sub-sampling and found stable performance when using at least 100 samples for model training (Extended Data Fig. 5g). The elastic net model performed similarly well in a withheld validation cohort collected at an independent institution (AUROC = 0.95; Fig. 3l,m). Performance of the elastic net classifier was statistically similar to the enrichment score framework in the training cohort (P = 0.27), but improved performance in the validation cohort (P = 0.001). Therefore, when sufficient samples are available, machine learning-based approaches can also be used to train robust classifiers for RARE-seq.

> Fig. 3 | Detection of ctRNA in plasma from patients with NSCLC. a, Differential expression between LUAD cfRNA (n = 139) and non-cancer cfRNA (n = 50). Significance was determined using Wald test and Benjamini–Hochberg correction. b, Average expression of genes enriched in LUAD cfRNA in LUAD tumours (n = 385) and meta-reference cfRNA (n = 15). Comparison used two-sided Wilcoxon test (****P ≤ 2.2 × 10−16). c, GSEA using PanglaoDB (Ref: 12) cell-type signatures. Top 15 positive and negative types are shown, and the dashed line delineates significant enrichment. d, Enrichment scores for the LUAD Sig (n = 72 genes) in cfRNA from patients with LUAD (n = 139), risk-matched controls (n = 26) and healthy controls (n = 24). e, Sensitivity of LUAD cfRNA enrichment score detection, summarized by stage. Detection threshold achieving 95% or more specificity was used. Error bars represent the 95% binomial confidence interval. Stages were compared using Spearman correlation. f, Relationship between LUAD cfRNA enrichment score and ctDNA allele frequency (AF) in matched plasma (n = 38). ND, not detected. g, Percentage of matched samples with detectable ctRNA and/or ctDNA. h, Oncoprint of variants detected in cfRNA from patients with stage IV LUAD with one or more tissue-adjudicated or ctDNA-adjudicated variants and controls. i, Percentage of samples with one or more variants detected in cfRNA. j, Percentage of known variants detected in cfRNA. k, Relationship between EGFR expression and variant detection for samples with known EGFR variants. l, Probability of lung cancer from the elastic net (EN) model (for training, n = 96 LUAD and 47 control; for validation, n = 52 LUAD and 46 control). Detection threshold achieving 95% or more specificity in the training cohort was used (shown as dashed line). m, Receiver operating characteristic curves summarizing detection using LUAD cfRNA enrichment score and elastic net probabilities. Area under the curve (AUC) was compared with DeLong’s test. CV, cross-validation. In the boxplots, the centre line denotes the median, the box contains the IQR, and the whiskers denote extrema no farther than 1.5× IQR.

### Subsection: Identifying resistance to EGFR therapies

One of our motivations for developing RARE-seq was the hypothesis that in addition to identifying emergent somatic mutations mediating resistance, ctRNA analysis might allow detection of non-genetic therapeutic resistance mechanisms, such as histological transformation (Ref: 19). To explore this question, we focused on patients with EGFR-mutant LUAD treated with EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs). Acquired resistance to EGFR TKIs is caused by heterogenous mechanisms, including both EGFR-dependent mechanisms (such as secondary point mutations in the EGFR gene) and EGFR-independent mechanisms (such as bypass pathway activation or histological transformation) (Ref: 20,21). We profiled plasma samples from 10 patients with stage IV EGFR-mutant LUAD who had received osimertinib after progressing on at least one previous EGFR TKI and who then developed resistance to osimertinib. The mechanism of resistance was determined by tissue biopsy at the time of progression, revealing small-cell histological transformation in four patients, MET gene amplifications in three patients, and emergent C797S point mutations in EGFR in another three patients (Fig. 4a). For each patient, blood was collected before the start of osimertinib and at one or more timepoints after progression (n = 24 timepoints total).
Histological transformation to small-cell lung cancer (SCLC) was evaluated using a SCLC signature (SCLC Sig) defined using public tumour gene expression data (Ref: 18,22). The signature comprised 73 genes, including canonical SCLC markers such as ASCL1, NEUROD1 and INSM1. At time of radiological progression, the SCLC Sig was detected in three of four (75%) patients with biopsy-proven histological transformation (Fig. 4b). For one of these patients, we also analysed a plasma sample collected 274 days after the initiation of SCLC-targeted chemotherapy (for example, carboplatin/etoposide) and observed decreased SCLC cfRNA enrichment score, suggesting treatment response of the small cell component (Fig. 4c).
We next assessed the performance of RARE-seq for identifying genetic mechanisms of resistance. To detect MET pathway activation caused by MET amplification using ctRNA, we developed a MET amplification signature (METamp Sig) containing nine genes differentially expressed in three EGFR-mutant NSCLC cell lines with acquired resistance due to MET amplification (Methods). The METamp Sig was detected in two of three (67%) patients with biopsy-proven MET amplification (Fig. 4d). Of note, we observed a decreased METamp cfRNA enrichment score in one patient after the MET inhibitor savolitinib was added to osimertinib, coincident with a partial response by imaging (Fig. 4e). We then identified EGFRC797S-mutant reads in two of three (67%) patients known to have acquired this mutation based on tumour DNA or ctDNA analysis (Fig. 4f,g). These resistance mechanisms were not detected in any pre-resistance timepoint, confirming the specificity of our approach (Supplementary Table 6). Together, these results demonstrate proof of concept that RARE-seq can identify both non-genetic and genetic mechanisms underlying EGFR TKI resistance, including histological transformation, which is not detectable by mutation-based ctDNA approaches.

> Fig. 4 | Detecting mechanisms of resistance to EGFR TKIs in cfRNA. a, Summary of the EGFR TKI cohort (n = 10 patients, n = 24 timepoints). Mechanisms of resistance were determined via tissue biopsy. C797S, EGFRC797S SNV; METamp, MET amplification; tSCLC, histological transformation to SCLC. The illustrations of the plasma tubes and tissue block were created using BioRender (https://biorender.com). b, Enrichment scores for SCLC Sig (n = 73 genes) in patients with biopsy-proven tSCLC. The threshold representing 95% or more specificity in controls is shown by the dashed line. c, Vignette of a patient with tSCLC after EGFR TKI treatment. The left axis depicts the enrichment or elastic net scores, and the right axis depicts the allele frequency (AF) of tumour-adjudicated variants detected in cfRNA. PD, progressive disease; PR, partial response; SD, stable disease. d, Enrichment score for a METamp Sig (n = 9 genes) in patients with biopsy-proven MET amplification. The threshold representing 95% or more specificity in controls is shown by the dashed line. e, Vignette of a patient with emergence of MET amplification after EGFR TKI treatment. f, EGFRC797S allele frequency in cfRNA from patients with biopsy-proven C797S mutation. g, Vignette of a patient with emergence of a EGFRC797S mutation after EGFR TKI treatment. In the boxplots, the centre line denotes the median, the box contains the IQR, and the whiskers denote extrema no farther than 1.5× IQR.

### Subsection: Tissue-of-origin determination in ctRNA

We also wanted to test whether the same RARE-seq assay could be used to detect other cancer types. We therefore analysed plasma samples from patients with three types of other carcinomas: pancreatic adenocarcinoma (PAAD; n = 10 patients with stage IV), prostate adenocarcinoma (PRAD; n = 1 patient with stage III and n = 8 patients with stage IV) and liver hepatocellular carcinoma (LIHC; n = 3 patients with stage I, n = 5 patients with stage II and n = 2 patients with stage IV). Cancer-specific gene signatures were generated for each cancer type and evaluated using the cfRNA enrichment score framework (Supplementary Table 7). Sensitivity of cancer-specific cfRNA enrichment score detection was 80%, 90% and 100% for LIHC, PAAD and PRAD, respectively, after adjusting for multiple hypothesis testing (Fig. 5a). Given that cancer-specific signatures were detectable in multiple cancer types, we next wanted to explore whether ctRNA analysis can distinguish between cancer types. The ability to identify the TOO of a malignancy non-invasively could be helpful for patients with advanced-stage cancers of unknown primary who have no clinically detected primary tumour. Thus, we generated additional cancer signatures capturing genes uniquely expressed in each cancer compared with all other cancers, as well as to cfRNA from patients without cancer (cancer-TOO signatures). TOO was assessed for any advanced-stage cancer sample detected by at least one cancer-specific signature. The top predicted TOO was correct 89% of the time and the top two predicted TOOs identified the correct tumour type 96% of the time (Fig. 5b,c). Thus, cfRNA analysis could potentially enable non-invasive TOO identification.

### Subsection: Application to non-malignant conditions

We also explored several potential applications of RARE-seq beyond oncological settings, by first testing whether cfRNA expression might reveal lung injury caused by benign pulmonary conditions. We profiled cfRNA from 3 patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disorder, 7 patients with active COVID-19 infections (n = 13 timepoints) and 19 patients admitted to the intensive care unit with evidence of pulmonary injury (including 9 patients with acute respiratory distress syndrome). Across these diverse pulmonary conditions, lung-derived cfRNA was detectable in 49% of patients using a normal lung gene expression signature derived from GTEx (Ref: 16) (Fig. 5d and Supplementary Table 8). By contrast, among adult participants without known pulmonary conditions (n = 42), lung cfRNA was detected in 14%, with detection being significantly more frequent in current smokers than in former- or never-smokers (45.5% versus 3.6%, P = 0.004; Fig. 5e). This result suggests that lung cfRNA is induced by ongoing injury to pulmonary epithelium caused by recent exposure to tobacco smoke (Ref: 23,24). Conversely, in patients with known pulmonary conditions, detection was significantly greater in those who were on a ventilator at the time of blood collection (Fig. 5f), probably reflecting the severity of the pulmonary condition as well as insult to pulmonary epithelium from mechanical ventilation (Ref: 25). Thus, cfRNA analysis may have utility for assessment of benign conditions involving acute and chronic patterns of tissue injury.
Separately, given the rapidly growing interest in RNA-based vaccines and therapeutics, we explored whether RARE-seq might have utility for simultaneously tracking RNA-based treatments and their effects on the host. To do so, we longitudinally profiled plasma from five individuals receiving mRNA-based COVID-19 vaccinations (n = 20 timepoints). We performed RARE-seq on these samples using the RAG capture panel supplemented with baits targeting the sequence of the mRNA vaccine (Extended Data Fig. 3c). Vaccine RNA was detectable immediately after vaccination and persisted for up to 6 weeks after injection (Fig. 5g). Host genes that were differentially expressed after vaccination were enriched for inflammatory responses, interferon-γ responses and cytokine signalling, reflecting immune activation in response to vaccination (Fig. 5h). Vaccine RNA and host immune response genes were both highest in the first week post-vaccination (Fig. 5i). These results highlight the potential of RARE-seq for simultaneously measuring pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic aspects of RNA-based therapies.

> Fig. 5 | Exploring additional potential applications of RARE-seq. a, Sensitivity of detection of LIHC cfRNA (n = 10), PAAD (n = 10) and PRAD (n = 9) enrichment scores using the respective cancer-specific signatures. Detection thresholds achieving 90% or more specificity after multiple hypothesis correction were used. Error bars represent the 95% binomial confidence interval. b, Accuracy of TOO determination using cancer-TOO signatures in samples detected by at least one cancer-specific signature (n = 105). The shading depicts enrichment score rank for the true cancer type. c, Confusion matrix comparing predicted and true cancer type. d, Enrichment score for normal lung tissue signature (n = 5 genes) in cfRNA from patients with benign pulmonary conditions (n = 77 timepoints from 71 individuals). ARDS, acute respiratory distress syndrome; COPD, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease; COVID, COVID-19 infection; ICU, intensive care unit. e, Relationship between normal lung enrichment score and smoking history. Samples without known pulmonary conditions were compared using two-sided Wilcoxon test (n = 42, P = 0.00095). f, Relationship between normal lung enrichment score and ventilator status. Samples with known pulmonary conditions were compared using two-sided Wilcoxon test (n = 35 timepoints from 29 individuals, P = 0.0061). g, Timecourse depicting COVID-19 mRNA vaccine expression in cfRNA (n = 20 timepoints from 5 individuals). Data are presented as mean log fold change above baseline ± s.d. h, GSEA of DEGs in post-vaccination timepoints. DEGs were determined by a likelihood ratio test, and GSEA significance was determined using fgsea (Methods). The top ten MSigDb Hallmark sets are displayed (Ref: 37-39). DN, down. i, Heatmap showing COVID-19 mRNA vaccine and DEGs in post-vaccination timepoints. Expression was centred and scaled using pre-vaccination levels. In the boxplots, the centre line denotes the median, the box contains the IQR, and the whiskers denote extrema no farther than 1.5× IQR.

# TASK: Scientific Reasoning Extraction

## Overview

Analyze the provided scientific paper excerpt. Your task is to act as a scientific logician and extract the reasoning structures the authors use to construct their narrative. You will identify three distinct types of logical triplets: **Type Q1 (Inquiry Logic)** and **Type Q2 (Discovery Logic)** and **Type Q3 (Control Logic)**, each contains subtype(s) defined below. 

Extracting all logical structures into subtypes of Q1, Q2, and Q3.

## Definitions of Logical Structures

### Type Q1: Inquiry Logic (Experimental Setup)

**Concept:** This represents the author's planning phase. It connects a gap in knowledge to a specific action.

**Logic Flow:** 
- **Q1.1:** *Research Question/Objective [main_content] + Available Resources/Justification [context] → Operational Step [outcome]*
- **Q1.2:** *Hypothesized mechanism [main_content] + Available Resources/Justification [context] → Operational Step [outcome]*

| Component | Description | Example |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| `main_content` (Q1.1) | The specific research question, knowledge gap, or objective driving the immediate action. It describes *what* the authors want to understand. | "To determine whether CREM functions as a negative regulator in CAR-NK cells" |
| `main_content` (Q1.2) | The hypothesized mechanism, causal relationship, or working model being tested. It describes what the authors think might be happening. | "We examined how the confined invasive state affects therapeutic response, hypothesizing that the HMGB2-high neuronal state induced by confinement may promote drug tolerance"|
| `context` | The background information, prior availability of data, or existing model systems that make the experiment feasible or relevant. For Q1.2, this may also include known biological systems or pathways that motivate the proposed mechanism. This justifies *why* this specific approach was chosen. | "Given the established function of calcium as an activator of PKA (Ref: 32,33)" |
| `outcome` | The actual methodological step, assay, or analysis performed to address the objective (Q1.1) or to test the hypothesis (Q1.2). | "we used CRISPR–Cas9 to KO CREM in two CAR-NK cell models" |

---

### Type Q2: Discovery Logic (Interpretation)

**Concept:** This represents the author's synthesis phase. It connects raw data or existing biological understanding to new biological insights.

**Logic Flow:** 
- **Q2.1:** *Research Question [main_content] + Established Theory [context] → New Insight [outcome]*
- **Q2.2:** *Mechanism [main_content] + Established Theory [context] → New Insight [outcome]*
- **Q2.3:** *Empirical Evidence [main_content] + Established Theory [context] → Confirmed Insight [outcome]*
- **Q2.4:** *Empirical Evidence [main_content] + Established Theory [context] → Hypothesized Insight [outcome]*

| Component | Description | Example |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| `main_content` (Q2.1) | The research question or objective that the author want to understand. It is used as a starting point to generate a possible mechanistic explanation when combined with prior knowledge. | "To investigate how mechanical confinement induces HMGB2 upregulation" |
| `main_content` (Q2.2) | The hypothesized mechanism, causal relationship, or working model being tested. | "mTOR signalling has a key role downstream of taurine in leukaemia cells.  (Fig. 3a–d)" |
| `main_content` (Q2.3 or Q2.4) | The objective data points, statistical results, or morphological descriptions generated *specifically* in this study. It describes *what* was seen. | "CREM KO significantly enhanced the cytotoxicity of CAR-IL-15 NK cells in long-term cultures (Fig. 3a–d)" |
| `context` | Established biological rules, physical laws, or citations from external literature that act as a "lens" through which the research question can be addressed (Q2.1), the mechanism can be generalized, refined, or extended (Q2.2), or the raw data is viewed (Q2.3 and Q2.4). | "These patterns mirror epigenetic signatures associated with long-lived memory T cells (Ref: 40)" |
| `outcome` | The novel conclusion, hypothesis, or meaningful interpretation derived from combining the observation with the context. It describes *what it implies* for the biological system. | "This suggests that CREM acts as an inhibitory checkpoint downstream of IL-15 stimulation" |

**Note:** 
- Use Q2.3 when the authors present a strongly supported conclusion in `outcome` (e.g., "demonstrates", "confirms", "establishes").
- Use Q2.4 when the authors present a tentative interpretation or hypothesis in `outcome` (e.g., "suggests", "may", "could").

---

### Type Q3: Control Logic (Research Question)
**Concept:** This represents the author's effort to confirm a mechanism or the validity of a therapeutic strategy. It formulates questions or experimental directions to eliminate confounding factors, test alternative explanations, or assess robustness.

**Logic Flow:** 
- **Q3.1:** *Proposed Mechanism/Insight [main_content] + Established Theory [context] → Control/Validation Question [outcome]*

| Component | Description | Example |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| `main_content` | The hypothesized mechanism, which can be a biological mechanism or effectiveness of a therapeutic strategy. | "It is therefore possible that non-genetic approaches using small-molecule inhibitors or gene silencing may identify a therapeutic window for TAUT targeting in human cells." |
| `context` | Established biological principles, known pathways, or prior findings that introduce potential confounders, alternative explanations, or limitations. | "These patterns mirror epigenetic signatures associated with long-lived memory T cells (Ref: 40)" |
| `outcome` | A research question or validation objective designed to test the validity or limitations of the proposed mechanism. It describes what needs to be ruled out or confirmed. | "We therefore tested the impact of TAUT inhibition on growth and proliferation of normal human HSPCs as well as patient-derived AML cells." |


---

## Extraction Rules

### Rule 1: Exhaustive Coverage
Every sentence or phrase from any results subsection must belong to `main_content`, `context`, or `outcome` in a Q1’s or Q2’s or Q3’s subtype. Sentences are rarely redundant—if you're tempted to skip one, reconsider where it fits.

### Rule 2: Verbatim Extraction
Extract text exactly as it appears in the excerpt:
- **Include** figure/table references (e.g., "Fig. 1a") as they indicate evidence
- **Include** reference markers (e.g., "Ref: 14") as they indicate literature support
- **Do NOT** correct grammar, rephrase, truncate mid-sentence, use "..." to shorten the text, or any other measures that compromise the exact extraction of the text.

### Rule 3: Marking Missing Components
Use `(missing)` ONLY when the text contains no explicit statement for that component:
- If authors state a result without citing literature or principles → `context` = `(missing)`
- If authors state a result without interpretation → `outcome` = `(missing)`
**Note:** The `(main_content)` is rarely missing, if you see `(context)` or `(outcome)` standalone, please recheck surrounding phrases/sentences, or consider being another triplet type. 
**Important:** Do NOT infer from general scientific knowledge. Only extract what is written.

### Rule 4: One Logical Unit Per Triplet
- Extract ONE triplet per logical unit (one goal → one method (may include multiple experiments), or one observation → one interpretation)
- If multiple observations **collectively** support ONE conclusion, group them into one Q2 triplet with combined `main_content`
- If a single experiment yields multiple **independent** observations, create separate Q2 triplets for each

### Rule 5: Negative Results Are Observations
Null findings (e.g., "X did NOT show Y", "there was no significant difference") ARE valid observations and should be extracted as Q2 `main_content`. Their interpretive significance belongs in `outcome`.

---
## Handling Special Cases

### Summary/Conclusion Statements

When authors provide concluding statements that infer a mechanism using multiple results (e.g., "Collectively...", "Together, these data suggest...", "These findings indicate..."):

**Option A (Preferred):** Create a final Q2 triplet for the subsection where:
- `main_content` = the key observations being synthesized (may repeat/combine prior observations)
- `context` = `(missing)` unless literature is cited
- `outcome` = the synthesis statement

**Option B:** If the conclusion directly follows a single observation, attach it as the `outcome` of that Q2 triplet instead of creating a new one.

However, if the concluding statements simply summarize findings rather than infer a biological understanding, treat it as a separate result. 

### Compound Sentences

If a sentence contains elements of both Q1 and Q2 (e.g., "Given X, we did Y and found Z"):
1. Determine the sentence's PRIMARY function:
- If primarily setting up an experiment → Q1
- If primarily reporting data → Q2
- If primarily resulting in a control/validation step → Q3
2. If truly balanced, split into separate triplets
3. The secondary elements can inform the appropriate field (e.g., a brief result mention in a Q1 can inform that the method was successful)

### Transitional Phrases

Pure transitions like "We next examined..." or "We also investigated..." should be:
- Incorporated into the Q1 `main_content` if they express a goal
- Incorporated into the Q2/Q3 `outcome` if they logically follows a previous result or interpretation 

---

## What NOT to Extract as Separate Triplets

- Figure legends or detailed panel descriptions (unless they contain interpretive claims)
- Repeated statements of the same finding in different words (consolidate into one triplet)
- Background information in the Introduction (only extract from Results sections)

---
## Output Format


```json
{
  "paper_title": "Title of the paper",
  "extractions": [
    {
      "subsection": "Name of subsection",
      "triplets": [
        {
          "type": "Q1",
          "main_content": "The research question or goal (verbatim) OR (missing)",
          "context": "Background/justification for approach (verbatim) OR (missing)",
          "outcome": "The method/analysis performed (verbatim) OR (missing)"
        },
        {
          "type": "Q2",
          "main_content": "The empirical observation (verbatim) OR (missing)",
          "context": "Literature or established principle (verbatim) OR (missing)",
          "outcome": "The interpretation/conclusion (verbatim) OR (missing)"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```
---

# PAPER EXCERPT:
# Custom CRISPR–Cas9 PAM variants via scalable engineering and machine learning

## Abstract

Engineering and characterizing proteins can be time-consuming and cumbersome, motivating the development of generalist CRISPR–Cas enzymes (Ref: 1-4) to enable diverse genome-editing applications. However, such enzymes have caveats such as an increased risk of off-target editing (Ref: 3,5,6). Here, to enable scalable reprogramming of Cas9 enzymes, we combined high-throughput protein engineering with machine learning to derive bespoke editors that are more uniquely suited to specific targets. Through structure–function-informed saturation mutagenesis and bacterial selections, we obtained nearly 1,000 engineered SpCas9 enzymes and characterized their protospacer-adjacent motif (PAM) (Ref: 7) requirements to train a neural network that relates amino acid sequence to PAM specificity. By utilizing the resulting PAM machine learning algorithm (PAMmla) to predict the PAMs of 64 million SpCas9 enzymes, we identified efficacious and specific enzymes that outperform evolution-based and engineered SpCas9 enzymes as nucleases and base editors in human cells while reducing off-targets. An in silico-directed evolution method enables user-directed Cas9 enzyme design, including for allele-selective targeting of the RHOP23H allele in human cells and mice. Together, PAMmla integrates machine learning and protein engineering to curate a catalogue of SpCas9 enzymes with distinct PAM requirements, motivating a shift away from generalist enzymes towards safe and efficient bespoke Cas9 variants.

## Introduction

The properties of CRISPR–Cas enzymes have been extensively engineered for genome-editing applications (Ref: 8,9). The first step of target recognition by DNA-targeting Cas enzymes involves the readout of the PAM, a short sequence motif (approximately 2–4 nucleotides) (Ref: 7), which then initiates guide RNA (gRNA) pairing and cleavage (Ref: 10-12) (Fig. 1a). Streptococcus pyogenes Cas9 (SpCas9) requires a 3′ NGG PAM (Ref: 12,13), restricting its use to PAM-encoding genomic sequences. For many genome-editing applications, precise positioning of the Cas enzyme is critical (for example, for allele-specific editing, base editing and modifying regulatory elements, among others; Extended Data Fig. 1a and Supplementary Note 1). For this reason, SpCas9 variant enzymes (Ref: 1-4,14,15) and other Cas orthologues (Ref: 16-20) capable of recognizing non-canonical PAMs have been engineered. These enzymes can be categorized into two main classes: altered PAM enzymes that shift the PAM preference away from NGG, and relaxed PAM enzymes that expand editing to new PAMs while retaining activity against NGG (Extended Data Figs. 1b–d and Supplementary Note 2). Relaxation of the PAM is the most common engineering trajectory, leading to the creation of generalist enzymes (Ref: 1-4). PAM-relaxed enzymes are convenient because a single enzyme can be used across many applications; however, expanded access to the genome results in poorer specificity than wild-type SpCas9 (Ref: 3,5,6,21) and extended genome searching can result in slower cleavage kinetics (Ref: 22). By constrast, PAM-selective enzymes should enable efficient on-target editing while minimizing off-targets, yet few have been developed. A large collection of selective enzymes that target different PAMs and collectively enable broad access to the genome would be an optimal solution for efficient, safe and versatile genome editing (Fig. 1b). Generating such a catalogue of altered PAM enzymes would require overcoming certain challenges associated with strategies typically used to engineer Cas enzymes (Ref: 1-4,14,17-23). Predicting the functional effect of multiple simultaneous mutations remains challenging in the context of rational protein engineering, and experimental selection strategies that enable directed evolution can be laborious and time consuming, often yielding few engineered enzymes per evolution campaign (Supplementary Note 2). We therefore turned to machine learning to augment our experimental engineering strategies. Computational predictions can be used to screen larger numbers of enzymes bearing more diverse combinations of amino acid substitutions than experimental methods alone, increasing the probability of identifying optimal enzymes (Ref: 23-30). Machine learning has been applied to engineer various proteins, including antibodies (Ref: 31-34), zinc fingers (Ref: 35-37), adeno-associated virus capsid tropism (Ref: 38-40), and to prioritize activity-altering mutations for Staphylococcus aureus Cas9 (SaCas9) (Ref: 26) or SpCas9 (Ref: 41). We hypothesized that with sufficient training data, the relationship between SpCas9 amino acid sequence and PAM recognition should also be learnable. The ability to accurately predict the PAM of a Cas enzyme based on amino acid sequence would enable systematic exploration of a larger sequence space accessible only by simultaneously mutating multiple residues involved in PAM interaction. Here we undertook a scalable protein engineering campaign to deeply profile the contribution of SpCas9 PAM-interacting (PI) domain amino acids to the PAM requirement. By generating experimental PAM profiles for hundreds of engineered SpCas9 enzymes, we produced training data for a machine learning model, PAMmla, to relate amino acid sequence to enzyme function. PAMmla can predict PAM variant enzymes with tuneable activities and specificities, which we tested in proof-of-concept experiments in human cells and mice. Together, integration of machine learning with protein engineering enabled the development of a collection of bespoke PAM-selective enzymes, towards the optimization of highly safe and effective CRISPR genome-editing technologies.

## Results

### Subsection: Scalable characterization of SpCas9 PAM variants

To generate datasets necessary to train a machine learning model to link protein sequence to function (PAM specificity), we sought to engineer and characterize a sufficiently large collection of novel SpCas9 enzymes with altered PAM preferences. We generated a saturation mutagenesis library of SpCas9 variant enzymes using a structure–function-informed approach based on SpCas9 structures (Ref: 10) and previous engineering efforts (Ref: 3,14,42,43), simultaneously mutating six amino acid residues within the SpCas9 PI domain, resulting in a library with theoretical complexity of 64 million enzymes (the SpCas9(6AA) library) (Fig. 1c, Extended Data Fig. 2a and Supplementary Note 3). We selected residues D1135, S1136, G1218, E1219, R1335 and T1337 for saturation mutagenesis, and individual enzymes within the library are hereafter named according to amino acid identities at these six positions. These six amino acids modulate specificity for the 3rd and 4th PAM bases in the context of the engineered enzymes SpCas9-VRER, SpCas9-VRQR and SpG (Ref: 3,14,42) (Supplementary Note 3). Expansion of our library to also mutate R1333, the residue that contacts the second guanine of the SpCas9 PAM (Ref: 10), largely abrogated enzyme activity (Supplementary Note 3). We therefore focused our efforts on altering the 3rd and 4th positions of the PAM. To identify SpCas9 PAM variant enzymes with activity on non-canonical PAMs, we performed an extensive set of bacterial-based positive selection assays (Ref: 14,44) to select for enzymes that are capable of cleaving target sites bearing each of the 16 possible NGNN PAMs, which have a fixed G nucleotide at the second position and that vary at the third and fourth positions (Extended Data Fig. 2b,c and Supplementary Note 4). Across the 16 experiments using the SpCas9(6AA) library, we obtained 634 unique SpCas9 enzymes that survived the selections (Supplementary Table 1). We observed PAM-dependent enrichment of amino acids across the enzymes depending on the 3rd or 4th nucleotide of the PAM used for the selection (Extended Data Fig. 2c). Next, we linked amino acid sequence to PAM specificity for the 634 recovered SpCas9 enzymes. To identify the amino acid substitutions in the SpCas9 enzymes, we performed an arrayed plasmid sequencing method for rapid and inexpensive whole open reading frame identification based on multiplex PCR (Ref: 45) (Supplementary Fig. 1). The PAM specificities of each of the 634 SpCas9 enzymes were then characterized using a high-throughput PAM determination assay (Ref: 46) (HT-PAMDA; Fig. 1c,d, Supplementary Table 1 and Supplementary Fig. 2). HT-PAMDA comprehensively measures rate constants (k) of nuclease cleavage for an enzyme across a library of substrates encoding all possible PAMs, providing kinetic data to quantify the global PAM profile of an enzyme. HT-PAMDA experiments revealed that the bacterial selections performed using the SpCas9(6AA) library identified enzymes capable of recognizing each of the 16 NGNN PAMs (Fig. 1e and Supplementary Fig. 2). However, few enzymes were most active on the specific PAM that they were selected on (Supplementary Figs. 2 and 3), highlighting a limitation of the bacterial-based selection approach. Clustering the enzymes by their HT-PAMDA-determined PAM profiles revealed eight main classes of PAM variant enzymes that were each associated with specific amino acid substitutions (Fig. 1d). The two most common classes of SpCas9 enzymes represented in our dataset from the SpCas9(6AA) library selections were those with relaxed NGN PAM (similar to SpG or SpCas9-NG) or NGG PAM (similar to wild-type SpCas9) but with up to four amino acid substitutions compared with previously described enzymes (Supplementary Figs. 4 and 5). Most interestingly, our bacterial-based selections also revealed various enzymes with preferences for non-canonical nucleotides in the 3rd and 4th positions of the PAM. In many cases, we observed a nucleotide preference at the 4th nucleotide of the PAM, extending the PAM (specifying three bases instead of two). A preference for an extended PAM may hold advantages for minimizing off-targets, as has been demonstrated for other previously described enzymes (Ref: 14). Together, these observations highlight the previously uncharacterized plasticity of the SpCas9 PI domain. Although our bacterial selection experiments led to the evolution of SpCas9 enzymes with novel PAM requirements, the most efficiently targeted PAM of an enzyme did not always correlate with the PAM on which that enzyme was selected (Fig. 1e and Supplementary Figs. 2 and 3). We therefore sought to rationally design more optimal PAM-selective enzymes by testing 'consensus' enzymes for each of the 16 NGNN PAMs, assessed by combining the most enriched amino acids at each of the six positions recovered from each selection (Extended Data Fig. 2c). When assessing the PAM requirements of the consensus enzymes using HT-PAMDA (Supplementary Fig. 6a), most enzymes did not efficiently target the PAM for which they were designed, with only 4 of 31 consensus enzymes exhibiting maximal efficiency against their intended PAMs (Fig. 1e and Supplementary Fig. 6b). The rationally designed consensus enzymes generally had weaker efficiencies than the enzymes obtained directly from bacterial selections (Fig. 1e and Supplementary Fig. 6a). These results indicate that mutations at these six positions do not contribute to PAM preference independently, but rather interact with one another in an epistatic manner; selections and rational design alone are thus insufficient to systematically obtain enzymes with desired PAMs. To approximate the fraction of the 64 million enzymes encoded with our SpCas9(6AA) library that retained editing activity on any PAM, we performed HT-PAMDA on 135 randomly chosen library members. Unexpectedly, we found that approximately 18% of the enzymes in the SpCas9(6AA) library were capable of editing on at least one PAM, many of them with non-canonical PAM preferences (Fig. 1f). This observation suggested that among the millions of possible combinations of residues in our six-position library, more than 10 million enzymes may be functional, the vast majority of which remain uncharacterized.

### Subsection: Learning PAM preference from amino acid sequence

As we could experimentally characterize only a small number of enzymes from the SpCas9(6AA) library (approximately 0.001%), and our data suggest that a substantial fraction of the enzymes may be active on at least one PAM (more than 15%; Fig. 1f), we envisioned that many additional enzymes with useful PAM requirements remained to be characterized. This, together with the observation that enriched amino acid substitutions were often neither necessary nor sufficient to predict the PAM preference of most enzymes, led us to seek a more systematic method to investigate the relationship between amino acid sequence and PAM specificity. We therefore sought to explore the entire fitness landscape of our SpCas9(6AA) library via machine learning. We utilized our two HT-PAMDA datasets as training data for a machine learning model, including functional enzymes derived from our bacterial selections on the 16 NGNN PAMs and random (non-selected) enzymes from the SpCas9(6AA) library. We reasoned that including some inactive enzymes in our training set would provide information about combinations of amino acids that lead to non-functional enzymes. To account for the imbalanced nature of our training data towards NGG-targeting enzymes and relaxed variants that are most active on NGTG PAMs (Fig. 1f), we assigned a label to each training example based on its most active PAM and randomly oversampled to balance across PAM classes. We trained machine learning models to predict the rate constant k on each of the 64 PAMs (positions 2–4) when provided with a 6AA sequence as an input (Fig. 2a), and compared the ability of different machine learning models to extrapolate to amino acid combinations not encountered in the training set. Linear regression, random forest and neural network models were tested in combination with different feature encodings as inputs to the model (one-hot encoding of each amino acid substitution; one-hot encoding of all single plus pairwise amino acid combinations; and Georgiev (Ref: 47), a physiochemical descriptor that can improve performance of some machine learning models for protein engineering (Ref: 25); Extended Data Fig. 3a). Model architectures and amino acid encoding were compared using an internal fivefold cross-validation (Extended Data Fig. 3a). The use of pairwise amino acid features was necessary for the linear and random forest, but not neural network, models to achieve generalizability to enzymes dissimilar from the training set (Hamming distance from training set of 4). The neural network model with one-hot encoding was the most generalizable while requiring the simplest input feature set; thus this model was named PAMmla and was used for subsequent analyses. We evaluated PAMmla on a test set comprising a random 20% of enzymes from our HT-PAMDA dataset that were held out from training, revealing accurate k predictions for unseen SpCas9 enzymes (Pearson’s r = 0.91; Fig. 2b). We also evaluated PAMmla using two additional random train test splits that showed similar correlations (both r = 0.92; Extended Data Fig. 3b), demonstrating that these results are consistent regardless of the allocation of enzymes into training and testing sets. The test set was further subdivided into progressively more challenging subsets containing enzymes with increasing numbers of mutations relative to the most similar enzyme in the training set, demonstrating generalizability to enzymes that are dissimilar to those on which the model was trained (Fig. 2c). Across different PAM classes, the model performed relatively consistently, indicating the ability to generate accurate predictions, including for PAM classes that had relatively few training examples (Extended Data Figs. 3c–f). PAMmla models trained with oversampling to balance PAM classes within the training set improved performance on under-represented PAM classes in the test set (Extended Data Fig. 3g). In addition to accurately predicting PAM preferences of active SpCas9 enzymes, PAMmla was also capable of distinguishing active from inactive enzymes with high accuracy (area under the receiver operating curve (AUROC) = 0.99; Fig. 2d). With 'inactive' enzymes defined using a cut-off of maximum rate constant of less than 10−4.3, PAMmla correctly classified enzymes as active versus inactive for 149 of 154 enzymes (97%) in the test set (Fig. 2e). We selected k less than 10−4.3 to define a non-targetable PAM since the HT-PAMDA k values for bona fide inactive enzymes are noisy between ks of 10−5 to 10−4.3 (Extended Data Figs. 3h,i and Supplementary Note 5). We tested the ability of PAMmla to recapitulate HT-PAMDA-determined PAM profiles of preexisting rationally designed or evolved SpCas9 PAM variant enzymes not included in the training or test sets. We were able to generate accurate PAM profiles for SpG, VRER and VRQR (Pearson’s r = 0.99, 0.71 and 0.93, respectively), each of which encodes two mutations relative to the closest member of the training set (Fig. 2f–h). We also predicted the PAM profile of xCas9, an enzyme that contains only a single PI domain mutation, E1219V, but additional mutations outside of the PI domain (Ref: 2). When predicting the impact of the single E1219V mutation, PAMmla produced a predicted PAM profile remarkably similar to the experimental data for xCas9 (Fig. 2i), supporting previous evidence that the single E1219V mutation was the major contributor to the altered PAM of xCas9 (Ref: 3).

### Subsection: Machine learning-assisted prediction of PAM variant enzymes

We then utilized PAMmla to discover novel PAM variant enzymes by predicting the PAM profiles for each of the 64 million possible enzymes in the SpCas9(6AA) library (Supplementary Fig. 7). The resulting enzymes were sorted by their predicted PAM profiles on activity alone (kPAM) or selectivity (kPAM/sum(kall_PAMs)) for each of the 16 NGNN PAMs (Fig. 3a). Among the top predicted enzymes for each criterion and PAM, we chose between 3 and 10 enzymes to experimentally validate PAMmla predictions using HT-PAMDA (for 281 enzymes; Fig. 3b, Supplementary Table 2 and Supplementary Fig. 8a–c). Among this group of enzymes, the PAMmla predictions for each PAM correlated with experimentally obtained ks (Pearson’s r = 0.90; Fig. 3c and Supplementary Figs. 8a–c and 9). Most of the top predicted enzymes encoded two or three mutations (and some up to four) relative to the most similar previously characterized variant (Fig. 3d), indicating that PAMmla can accurately predict the PAM requirements of enzymes dissimilar from the training set. Clustering the experimentally determined PAM profiles from the PAMmla-predicted enzymes revealed ten main clusters (Fig. 3b,e). Clusters comprised new enzymes with novel PAM requirements compared with those previously seen (for example, NGTC, NGCT, NGCM (M = A or C) and relaxed with NGG anti-preference), along with sequence-diversified examples of enzymes with PAM profiles similar to those obtained from bacterial selections (for example, NGG, NGN and NGC). These classes of enzymes provide new insight into combinations of amino acids within the SpCas9(6AA) library that enable useful PAM profiles. Sorting PAMmla-predicted enzymes for maximized efficiency on each of the 16 NGNN PAMs resulted in enzymes with higher k values on the PAM of interest than those obtained from bacterial selections (Fig. 3f). Although highly efficient enzymes with activity on a PAM of interest with k > 10−2.5 (similar to the maximum k of SpG) were rarely recovered from bacterial selections (less than 9% of enzymes), the majority of PAMmla-derived enzymes (56%) designed to maximize activity had k > 10−2.5 on the PAM for which they were sorted and had maximal efficiency on that PAM (Fig. 3f and Supplementary Fig. 9). Furthermore, when we sorted PAMmla predictions for selectivity on a PAM of interest, the resulting enzymes were more specific for the target PAM than enzymes derived from bacterial selections (Fig. 3g). Approximately 60% of PAMmla enzymes designed to maximize selectivity had a maximal experimentally determined k on the specific 4-nt PAM for which sorting was performed (with 91% of PAMmla enzymes having the intended PAM in the top four; Fig. 3g and Supplementary Fig. 9). By contrast, only 26% of enzymes from bacterial selections exhibited maximal activity on the PAM from which they were selected (Fig. 3g and Supplementary Fig. 3). When ranked among PAMmla predictions of 64 million enzymes, bacterial selection-derived enzymes ranked on average in the 95th percentile in terms of activity (outranked by 3.2 million enzymes) and the 70th percentile in terms of selectivity (outranked by 18.9 million enzymes; Supplementary Fig. 10a,b), highlighting the utility of PAMmla to predict superior enzymes versus those derived from experimental selections. Thus, PAMmla predictions can be used to obtain enzymes tailored to the PAM of interest rather than generalist enzymes with relaxed PAMs, which tend to be the most common outcome of experimental engineering methods. We investigated potential functional roles of the amino acid substitutions in PAMmla-predicted enzymes (Supplementary Note 6). Using shapely additive explanations (SHAP) (Ref: 48) analysis of machine learning feature importance (Extended Data Fig. 4) and structural modelling (Extended Data Fig. 5), we observed that mutations in PAMmla enzymes can be divided into three categories that either: (1) alter 3rd PAM position preference (Extended Data Fig. 5a–c); (2) alter 4th PAM position preference (Extended Data Fig. 5d–f); or (3) modify PAM recognition via base-independent interactions (Extended Data Fig. 5g–i). Generally, more selective PAMmla enzymes harboured mutations that form base-specific contacts with the DNA bases of the PAM, whereas PAMmla enzymes with broader PAM specificity shared nonspecific activity-potentiating mutations with previously described PAM-relaxed enzymes that form nonspecific contacts with the DNA backbone (Ref: 3,6). Many PAMmla enzymes shared combinations of mutations from both categories, potentially achieving a balance between specifying novel PAMs and retaining high activity.

### Subsection: Testing PAMmla-predicted enzymes in human cells

We tested the editing efficiencies of 22 PAMmla-generated enzymes in HEK 293T cells, prioritizing those with preferences for PAMs that have not been previously described in the literature: NGAT, NGCM (M = A or C), NGCN, NGTN, NGTC, NGDC (D = A, G or T) and NGTG (Extended Data Fig. 6a and Supplementary Fig. 8a–c). We also assessed 14 enzymes from the training set that were identified from our bacterial selection experiments. For both enzyme classes, predicted PAM requirements correlated well with their HT-PAMDA-determined profiles (Supplementary Fig. 11). Nuclease forms of these 36 enzymes were assessed for generating insertion or deletion mutations (indels) across 32 endogenous target sites in HEK 293T cells compared with SpG, which is an engineered SpCas9 nuclease enzyme variant capable of targeting sites with NGN PAMs (Ref: 3). In general, PAMmla enzymes exhibited similar or improved average editing efficiencies compared with SpG (Fig. 4a and Extended Data Fig. 6b). Although we only highlight a single enzyme from each PAM class (Fig. 4a), for each target site that we assessed, several PAMmla enzymes often achieved similar or greater editing efficiencies than SpG while maintaining a more selective PAM preference (Extended Data Fig. 6b and Supplementary Fig. 12a–g). In some cases, PAMmla enzymes were substantially more efficient at creating indels than the most similar variants from the training set. For instance, the PAMmla enzyme LWKYQS was capable of approximately 1.5-fold higher average editing efficiencies on sites with NGCM PAMs than its nearest neighbour from the training set, LWKYSS, which contains only one amino acid difference (Extended Data Fig. 6b, NGCM variants). Editing efficiencies varied by target site (Supplementary Fig. 12a–g), suggesting spacer-specific differences and thus the potential utility of testing multiple PAMmla enzymes for each target of interest. We also tested all PAMmla enzymes on a control site bearing an NGG PAM to assess remaining activity against the ancestral PAM (Fig. 4b and Supplementary Fig. 12h). PAMmla enzymes predicted to poorly target NGG exhibited greatly diminished activity on the control NGG PAM target site (Supplementary Fig. 12i), indicating that PAMmla can generate enzymes that minimize editing on the canonical PAM. Together, these observations demonstrate that PAMmla-predicted enzymes improve human cell-based editing efficiencies compared with SpG or those derived from bacterial selections, and that PAMmla can be used to generate enzymes with altered rather than relaxed PAM specificities. PAM variants are useful in the context of base editing since precise positioning of Cas9 enzymes over the target base is required (Ref: 49). For each of the seven PAM categories tested above, we therefore selected a single PAMmla-derived enzyme to assess whether they could be beneficial for A-to-G and C-to-T editing in the contexts of ABE8e (Ref: 50) and TadCBEd (Ref: 51) architectures, respectively. For ABE8e and TadCBEd enzymes, we observed efficient base editing in HEK 293T cells at three genomic loci harbouring the preferred PAM of each of the corresponding Cas9 variants (Fig. 4c,d, Extended Data Fig. 6c,d and Supplementary Figs. 13 and 14). The base-editing efficiencies of PAMmla-generated base editors were superior to the PAM-relaxed SpRY in both ABE8e and TadCBEd contexts. PAMmla enzymes were more active versus SpG as ABE8e constructs (Fig. 4c) and similarly efficient as TadCBEd constructs depending on the site (Fig. 4d). We additionally tested PAMmla enzymes on a therapeutic base edit for sickle cell disease, achieving comparable on-target editing and fewer bystander edits compared with a previously reported approach (Ref: 52) (Supplementary Fig. 15a–h and Supplementary Note 7). Together, these observations demonstrate that PAMmla-derived editors are generally more efficient as nucleases and base editors compared with PAM relaxed enzymes.

### Subsection: Specificity of PAMmla-predicted enzymes

SpCas9 enzymes with relaxed PAM requirements are more prone to off-target editing as they search a larger fraction of the genome and thus encounter a larger number of potential off-target sites (Ref: 3,6,21). By contrast, PAMmla-predicted enzymes with altered PAM requirements should encounter fewer potential off-targets due to their more limited targeting ranges. To test this hypothesis, we utilized a cell-based unbiased genome-wide off-target assay, GUIDE-seq2 (Ref: 53), to identify nuclease-cleaved off-target sites. We observed comparable or greater on-target editing using PAMmla enzymes compared with SpG and SpRY at the sites chosen for GUIDE-seq2 (Fig. 4e and Extended Data Fig. 7a), and yet the total number of off-target sites were reduced with PAMmla enzymes versus SpG and SpRY (ranging from 26% to 93% reduction compared with SpG, and 49% to 96% reduction compared with SpRY; Fig. 4f). PAMmla enzymes resulted in a higher proportion of on-to-off-target GUIDE-seq2 reads relative to SpG and SpRY for all sites, including those with modest (Fig. 4g) and high (Fig. 4h) numbers of off-target sites. Off-target sites detected for each PAMmla-predicted enzyme were largely also detected in the SpG or SpRY sample with the same single guide RNA (sgRNA), although some off-targets were specific to the altered PAM variant enzymes (Extended Data Fig. 7b and Supplementary Note 8). The PAMs observed at off-target sites for each PAMmla-derived enzyme reflected their cognate HT-PAMDA-determined PAMs, whereas the aggregate PAMs observed for SpG and SpRY were more relaxed (Extended Data Fig. 7c). These results demonstrate that PAMmla enzymes with altered PAMs can reduce genome-wide off-targets compared with PAM-relaxed enzymes. To verify the on-target and off-target benefits of PAMmla versus generalist enzymes in human patient-derived cells, we sought to correct a CYBB mutation in cells from an individual with X-linked chronic granulomatous disease (X-CGD). Patients with X-CGD are highly susceptible to invasive infections and hyperinflammation that results in substantial morbidity and early mortality. CYBB mutations may be correctable through ex vivo editing of haematopoietic stem cells (Ref: 54,55). Using patient-derived Epstein–Barr virus-transformed B cells harbouring the CYBBT362I mutation, we identified a target site with an NGAT PAM that positions the causative G-to-A mutation at position 8 within the protospacer (Fig. 4i) and electroporated mRNA encoding either ABE8e-SpG or the PAMmla-derived ABE8e-KWRQLC enzyme along with the A8 sgRNA. Both A-to-G base editors (ABEs) resulted in highly efficient levels of T362I correction (up to more than 90%) with minimal bystander editing (Fig. 4j). GUIDE-seq2 assessment of off-targets using the CYBBT362I-targeted sgRNA in HEK 293T cells revealed only a single low-likelihood off-target site detected when using the PAMmla-predicted KWRQLC enzyme compared with five off-target sites with SpG (Extended Data Fig. 7e), resulting in a superior on-to-off-target ratio for the PAMmla enzyme compared with the PAM-relaxed SpG (Fig. 4k).

### Subsection: PAMmla-enabled allele-specific editing in vivo

One potential application of PAMmla is to predict SpCas9 enzymes for allele-specific editing (Ref: 56) by maximizing activity on a PAM of interest while minimizing activity on another. Sorting the PAMmla predictions for all 64 million SpCas9(6AA) enzymes is cumbersome, so we developed an in silico-directed evolution (ISDE) approach that utilizes PAMmla predictions to computationally engineer SpCas9 variant enzymes in a stepwise manner (Extended Data Fig. 8a). ISDE enables prediction of optimal enzymes with greater computational efficiency, enabling rapid sorting by customizable fitness metrics (Extended Data Fig. 8b–d and Supplementary Note 9). As a proof of concept, we sought to utilize PAMmla-ISDE to evolve a customized editor to target the human rhodopsin (RHO) P23H mutation causative of retinitis pigmentosa (Ref: 57-59). As a dominant negative mutation, knockout of the mutant allele while leaving the wild-type allele intact can rescue function in heterozygous genotypes (Ref: 60,61). Single-nucleotide mutations can be difficult to target in an allele-specific manner via the sgRNA due to the propensity of SpCas9 to tolerate single-base-pair mismatches between the sgRNA and target DNA (Ref: 56,62). However, if a disease-causing point mutation generates a novel PAM, a high-specificity PAM proof-reading step may be exploited for allele-specific editing (Ref: 56,63,64). For allele-selective editing of RHOP23H, we utilized PAMmla-ISDE to design enzymes that target an NGTG PAM on the mutant allele and that minimize editing of NGGG on the wild-type allele (Fig. 5a). Neither wild-type SpCas9 nor SpG are useful, as SpCas9 should efficiently target the wild-type P23 allele and SpG should target both P23 and P23H alleles equally. We initiated PAMmla-ISDE starting with the wild-type SpCas9 sequence and evolved enzymes by sorting for maximized activity on NGTG PAMs while requiring a k < 10−3.7 on NGGG. The PAMmla-ISDE fitness plateaued after four cycles of mutagenesis and prediction (Fig. 5b,d and Supplementary Fig. 16a). The optimal enzyme, MRRWMR, was predicted by PAMmla to strongly target NGTG and weakly target NGGG (k = 10−2.1 and 10−3.7, respectively; Fig. 5b). MRRWMR was also the optimal enzyme determined via ISDE using SpG as a starting sequence and different parameters (Supplementary Fig. 16b). We also performed a stricter evolution via IDSE to further minimize targeting of NGGG, requiring a k < 10−4 on NGGG, which resulted in several enzymes including KRHWMR after four rounds of evolution (Fig. 5c,e and Supplementary Fig. 16c). Initial experiments with MRRWMR on three unrelated genomic sites harbouring NGTG PAMs achieved similar editing efficiencies compared with SpG, with minimal editing on a site bearing an NGGG PAM (Extended Data Fig. 9a). We then generated a heterozygous RHOP23H HEK 293T cell line to assess allele-selective editing on the target of interest (Supplementary Fig. 17a–f). We tested a total of four ISDE-derived enzymes from either campaign in the RHOP23H cell line and observed a preference for editing the mutant over the wild-type allele compared with SpG for all ISDE enzymes (Extended Data Fig. 9b). The MRRWMR enzyme led to the most efficient mutant allele disruption, and KRHWMR resulted in superior allele-specific discrimination with nearly undetectable editing of the wild-type allele in heterozygous and wild-type cells (Fig. 5f and Extended Data Fig. 9b,c). The ISDE-derived MRRWMR and KRHWMR enzymes exhibited approximately 2.5- and 40-fold preferences for mutant P23H over wild-type RHO alleles, respectively (Extended Data Fig. 9e). Use of other recently described Cas9 orthologue nucleases PrCas9 (Ref: 65), CoCas9 (Ref: 66) and GeCas9 (Ref: 66) harbouring PAM preferences that should target the P23H site failed to elicit detectable editing at the RHO locus (Supplementary Fig. 18a–c and Supplementary Note 10). Next, we performed off-target analyses via GUIDE-seq2 to investigate specificity improvements with the allele-selective PAMmla enzymes. On two unrelated genomic loci, we observed enhanced specificity with MRRWMR over SpG and SpRY (Extended Data Fig. 10a–c). When using the RHOP23H sgRNAs in a homozygous P23H cell line, both MRRWMR and KRHWMR also improved on-target specificity by minimizing off-target reads and the number of detected off-target sites compared with SpG and SpRY (Fig. 5g and Extended Data Fig. 10d–f). To examine potential structural determinants of the PAM-selective preferences of MRRWMR and KRHWMR, we modelled their mutations and investigated their contributions to PAMmla-predicted rates on NGTG and NGGG PAMs using SHAP (Fig. 5h–k and Extended Data Fig. 11a–e). For both enzymes, PAM selectivity for a 3rd PAM position T over G largely resulted from the E1219W and R1335M side chains forming a hydrophobic pocket, enabling discrimination via favourable hydrophobic interactions with the T3 methyl group of an NGTG PAM and unfavourable interaction with the polar G3 side chain of NGGG (Fig. 5h,i). SHAP analysis supports that these mutations positively affect recognition of NGTG and negatively affect recognition of NGGG (Fig. 5j,k and Extended Data Fig. 11b–e). Additional common mutations between the two PAMmla enzymes S1136R and T1337R contribute to PAM selectivity and/or potentiation of activity (Extended Data Fig. 11 and Supplementary Note 11). We assessed the in vivo editing efficiency and allele specificity of MRRWMR and KRHWMR in mouse retinas. Subretinal plasmid injections in humanized heterozygous WT-hRHO–GFP/P23H-hRHO–RFP mice at postnatal days 0–2 (P0–P2) were performed before in vivo electroporation. Analysis of on-target editing in transfected cells with MRRWMR revealed mean on-target efficiency of 37% on the P23H allele (up to 59%) and 7.6% on the wild-type allele, leading to 4.8-fold selectivity for editing the mutant allele (Fig. 5l and Extended Data Fig. 10h). Consistent with results in cells, KRHWMR resulted in lower in vivo editing (approximately 20%) but exhibited increased specificity for the P23H over wild-type allele (9.5-fold; Fig. 5l and Extended Data Fig. 10h). Together, these findings demonstrate how PAMmla-ISDE can predict novel enzymes for therapeutically relevant edits with no intervening engineering or evolution. PAMmla-nominated enzymes enable cell-based and in vivo allele-selective editing not possible with previously available SpCas9 enzyme variants or Cas9 orthologues.
